coredata.dc:title: In-situ detection of peroxynitrite in a single living cell with electrochemical attosyringe
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Shuai
Shi, Ji Fen
Gao, Ya Ting
Ye, Ming Jie
Qian, Ruo Can
Lv, Jian
Chen, Bin Bin
Li, Da Wei
coredata.dc:description: Peroxynitrite (ONOO−), a key reactive oxygen/nitrogen species, plays a crucial role in signal transduction and physiological homeostasis. However, achieving the determination of ONOO− in a single living cell remains a significant challenge. In this study, we have synthesized blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) with a specific response to ONOO− via a simple Schiff base crosslinking strategy. The fluorescence of these CDs is efficiently quenched upon interaction with ONOO− due to a strong nitration reaction, enabling highly sensitive and selective detection of ONOO− within a concentration range of 0.06–1.0 μM, with a detection limit as low as 30 nM. Notably, we have developed an electrochemical attosyringe by loading the CDs solution into a nanopipette, facilitating in-situ detection of ONOO− in a single living cell. This approach, which differs from conventional incubation methods, offers rapid infusion and ease of operation, presenting promising opportunities for advancing single-cell analysis and enhancing our understanding of cellular microenvironments.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Characterization of the CDs. A facile room temperature method is developed to prepare CDs via a Schiff base reaction by mixing tetrahydroxyquinone (TQ) and ethylenediamine (EDA), and the detailed synthesized method is described in supporting information. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1a) shows that the resulting CDs are non-crystalline with a disordered structure, and exhibit a relatively uniform size distribution ranging from 1.5 to 4.0 nm (Fig. 1b). The Raman spectrum (Fig. S1) displays two characteristic peaks at approximately 1300 cm\u22121 and 1580 cm\u22121, corresponding to the D band (disordered sp3-hybridized carbon) and G band (ordered sp2-hybridized carbon), respectively. The high intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 1.22 indicates a significant degree of disorder in the CDs [21,22], consistent with the result of TEM image. Due to the \u03C0-\u03C0\u2217 and the n-\u03C0\u2217 transition of the C[dbnd]C bond and C[dbnd]N bond [23,24], the obtained CDs show two characteristic absorption peaks at 260 and 345 nm (Fig. 1c). Additionally, the FL spectrum reveals a maximum emission at 440 nm when excited at 360 nm. The CDs also exhibit excitation-dependent FL in the excitation range of 320\u2013420 nm (Fig. 1d).The authors appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22176058), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (24DX1400200, 22ZR1416800, and 23ZR1416100), the Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities (B16017), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (222201717003) and Postdoctoral Foundation Project of Shenzhen Polytechnic University (6024331012K). We thank the Research Center of Analysis and Test of East China University of Science and Technology for help with the characterization.

The authors appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22176058), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (24DX1400200, 22ZR1416800, and 23ZR1416100), the Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities (B16017), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (222201717003) and Postdoctoral Foundation Project of Shenzhen Polytechnic University (6024331012K). We thank the Research Center of Analysis and Test of East China University of Science and Technology for help with the characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the proposed CDs. (a) TEM image, scale bar: 20 nm. (b) Particle size distribution. (c) UV absorption and FL spectra. (d) Emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths. (e) FT-IR spectrum. (f) XPS spectrum. (g) High-resolution C1s spectrum. (h) High-resolution N1s spectrum. (g) High-resolution O1s spectrum.

Detection and mechanism of ONOO− by CDs. (a) FL spectra of CDs in the presence of ONOO− solution with different concentrations. (b) Sensitivity of ONOO− detection. F0 represents FL intensity of CDs, F represents FL intensity of CDs after adding ONOO−. (c) FL spectra of CDs in the presence of interferences. (d) Selective detection. 1: ONOO−; 2: H2O2; 3: HClO; 4: 1O2; 5: •OH; 6: ROO•; 7: GSH; 8: Cys; 9: Na2SO3; 10: NaHSO3; 11: NaHCO3; 12: Na2CO3; 13: K+; 14: Mg2+; 15: Na+; 16: Ca2+. Concentration: 1 μM for ONOO−; 10 μM for 2–6; 100 μM for 7–16. Error bars represent ± S.D (n = 3). (e) High-resolution N1s spectra. (f) FT-IR spectra of CDs before and after adding ONOO−. (g) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs upon addition of increasing ONOO−. (h) FL lifetimes of CDs with different concentrations of ONOO−.

FL imaging of single cell injected with the CDs at different applied voltages and perfusion time. Left: the bright field and fluorescent images of single cells after injecting CDs through electrochemical attosyringe within 5 min. Right: the bright field and fluorescent images of single cells after withdrawing the electrochemical attosyringe. Scale bar: 20 μm.

FL images of CDs in a single HeLa cell under different conditions. First row, images of cell without pretreatment; second row, images of cell activated with SIN-1 (1 mM) for 2 h; third row, images of cell activated with LPS (5 μg/mL) and IFN-γ (1 μg/mL) for 2 h; and fourth row, images of cell activated with LPS (5 μg/mL) and IFN-γ (1 μg/mL) for 2 h, then incubated with TEMPO (300 μM) for 2 h; fifth row, images of cell preincubated with UA (200 μM) for 2 h before incubating SIN-1 (1 mM). Stage i: Microscopic images of HeLa cells injected with CDs for 5 min via electrochemical attosyringe. Stage ii: Microscopic images of the electrochemical attosyringe withdrawn from targeted HeLa cells. Stage i-P and Stage ii-P: the pseudocolor of FL images corresponding to Stage i and Stage ii, respectively. Scale bar: 20 μm.

(a) Schematic diagram of preparation of CDs. (b) The setup of electrochemical attosyringe based on a CDs-filled nanopipette. (c) The fluorescent changes of CDs before and after in-situ response to ONOO− in a single living cell.

head.ce:title: In-situ detection of peroxynitrite in a single living cell with electrochemical attosyringe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry
Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hoffmann Institute of Advanced Materials, Shenzhen Polytechnic University, 7098 Liuxian Blvd, Nanshan District, Shenzhen, 518055, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hoffmann Institute of Advanced Materials, Shenzhen Polytechnic University, 7098 Liuxian Blvd, Nanshan District, Shenzhen, 518055, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Peroxynitrite (ONOO−), a key reactive oxygen/nitrogen species, plays a crucial role in signal transduction and physiological homeostasis. However, achieving the determination of ONOO− in a single living cell remains a significant challenge. In this study, we have synthesized blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) with a specific response to ONOO− via a simple Schiff base crosslinking strategy. The fluorescence of these CDs is efficiently quenched upon interaction with ONOO− due to a strong nitration reaction, enabling highly sensitive and selective detection of ONOO− within a concentration range of 0.06–1.0 μM, with a detection limit as low as 30 nM. Notably, we have developed an electrochemical attosyringe by loading the CDs solution into a nanopipette, facilitating in-situ detection of ONOO− in a single living cell. This approach, which differs from conventional incubation methods, offers rapid infusion and ease of operation, presenting promising opportunities for advancing single-cell analysis and enhancing our understanding of cellular microenvironments.

An electrochemical attosyringe is constructed for in-situ imaging of ONOO− in a single living cell by infusing the carbon dots (CDs) solution into a quartz nanopipette.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Peroxynitrite (ONOO−), formed by the reaction of nitric oxide free radicals and superoxide anions, is a significant reactive oxygen/nitrogen species that acts as a signaling molecule and participates in various intracellular pathways [1–3]. Although ONOO− plays a crucial role in signal transduction, excessive levels can cause irreversible damage to key biological components, including DNA, proteins, and lipids [4–6]. In addition, abnormal levels of ONOO− are implicated in the pathogenesis of several diseases, such as inflammation, diabetes, neurological disorders, and cancer [7–9]. Therefore, accurate determination of ONOO− is critical for understanding its roles in physiological and pathological processes.

With advances in analytical techniques, a variety of ONOO− detection methods, including chemiluminescence [10,11], fluorescence (FL) [12], and electrochemical approaches [13–16], have been reported. Among these analytical methods, luminescent sensing techniques have demonstrated powerful capabilities in the determination of ONOO− due to their ease of use, low cost, and high sensitivity [17]. Additionally, luminescent probes enable real-time imaging of ONOO− in living cells with optical microscopes [18–20]. Despite this progress, conventional incubation methods limit precise imaging of ONOO− at the single-cell level, hindering our understanding of its specific cellular functions. Therefore, there is an urgent need for innovative approaches that allow high-resolution, single-cell imaging of ONOO−.

Herein, we report, for the first time, the successful imaging of ONOO− in a single living cell using carbon dots (CDs) integrated with an electrochemical attosyringe. The blue-emitting CDs, specifically responsive to ONOO−, are synthesized via a simple Schiff base crosslinking strategy at room temperature (Scheme 1 a). The resulting CDs exhibit good stability and excellent biocompatibility. Upon exposure to ONOO−, the blue FL of the CDs is quenched due to a strong nitration reaction, enabling highly sensitive and selective detection of ONOO−, with a detection limit of 30 nM. Importantly, by loading the CDs into a quartz nanopipette, we construct an electrochemical attosyringe capable of in-situ detection of ONOO− in a single living cell, without the need for conventional incubation (Scheme 1b and 1c). This innovative sensing platform not only provides high selectivity for ONOO− detection but also allows rapid in-situ analysis at the single-cell level, offering new insights into the role of ONOO− in cellular processes.

Characterization of the CDs. A facile room temperature method is developed to prepare CDs via a Schiff base reaction by mixing tetrahydroxyquinone (TQ) and ethylenediamine (EDA), and the detailed synthesized method is described in supporting information. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1 a) shows that the resulting CDs are non-crystalline with a disordered structure, and exhibit a relatively uniform size distribution ranging from 1.5 to 4.0 nm (Fig. 1b). The Raman spectrum (Fig. S1) displays two characteristic peaks at approximately 1300 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, corresponding to the D band (disordered sp3-hybridized carbon) and G band (ordered sp2-hybridized carbon), respectively. The high intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 1.22 indicates a significant degree of disorder in the CDs [21,22], consistent with the result of TEM image. Due to the π-π∗ and the n-π∗ transition of the CC bond and CN bond [23,24], the obtained CDs show two characteristic absorption peaks at 260 and 345 nm (Fig. 1c). Additionally, the FL spectrum reveals a maximum emission at 440 nm when excited at 360 nm. The CDs also exhibit excitation-dependent FL in the excitation range of 320–420 nm (Fig. 1d).

Subsequently, the structure of CDs is studied in detail. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum shows that the CDs primarily contain O–H/N–H (3489 cm−1), CN (1640 cm−1), C–N (1387 cm−1), and C–OH (1082 cm−1) groups (Fig. 1e) [25]. The existence of the CN bond confirms the occurrence of Schiff base crosslinking during the formation of CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. 1f) shows that the CDs are composed of carbon (285.9 eV), nitrogen (399.7 eV) and oxygen (531.0 eV), with atomic percentages of 58.66 %, 20.16 %, and 21.18 %, respectively (Table S1). In addition, high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 1g–i) confirm the presence of CN, CC, CO, C–N, and C–OH bonds, indicating the formation of a Schiff base structure in the CDs. These results reveal that CDs are formed by Schiff base crosslinking between TQ and EDA, with surfaces enriched in functional groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups.

Stability and cytotoxicity of the CDs. The stability of CDs is crucial for avoiding false-positive or negative results in biological imaging and chemical sensing. As shown in Fig. S2, the FL intensity of CDs remains nearly unchanged under continuous UV light exposure, indicating good resistance to photobleaching. Additionally, the CDs exhibit high stability in salty media, with no significant change in FL intensity even at NaCl concentrations as high as 4 M (Fig. S3). The FL intensity of CDs also remains stable across a pH range of 5.6–8.5 (Fig. S4). Subsequently, the influence of elevated temperature on the performance of CDs is also investigated. The results demonstrate that the FL intensity of CDs gradually decreases with increasing temperature (Fig. S5), which can be attributed to the temperature-dependent acceleration of non-radiative rate, consequently reducing the FL efficiency of CDs. Furthermore, no significant variation in FL intensity is observed in the CDs solution with or without deoxygenation, suggesting that environmental oxygen exerts minimal influence on the FL behavior of CDs (Fig. S6). The cytotoxicity of the CDs is further evaluated using the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. The results indicate that nearly 100 % of HeLa cells maintain their viability after incubation for 24 h with the CDs (0–100 μg/mL), indicating that the CDs are low toxicity (Fig. S7). Collectively, the CDs demonstrate excellent chemical stability and biocompatibility, making them suitable for sensing and imaging applications [26].

Detection and mechanism of ONOO− by CDs. The FL emission of CDs is efficiently quenched in the presence of ONOO− (Fig. 2 a), with an exceptionally rapid response, achieving completion within 30 s (Fig. S8). The FL intensity ratio ((F0–F)/F0) exhibits a strong linear relationship (R2 = 0.998) with ONOO− concentrations in the range of 0.06–1.0 μM, where F0 and F represent the FL intensity before and after adding ONOO−, respectively (Fig. 2b). The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 30 nM based on the 3σ/k method, which is highly competitive with other reported probes (Table S2). The CDs also exhibit excellent selectivity, as common reactive oxygen species (H2O2, HClO, •OH, 1O2, and ROO•) and potential interfering substances, such as glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), and various ions have negligible effects on FL intensity (Fig. 2c and d). These results demonstrate that the CDs are highly specific for ONOO− detection. Subsequently, the specific response mechanism of ONOO− are further investigated. ONOO− possesses high nitration capability, which allowing to react with amino groups. Upon exposure of CDs to ONOO−, a characteristic peak corresponding to –NO2 at 407.0 eV appears in the high-resolution XPS N1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) [27], confirming the nitration reaction has occurred on the surface of the CDs. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2f) further reveals new peaks at 1383, 1154, 948, and 857 cm−1 after ONOO− treatment [28], indicating the formation of nitro groups. Additionally, the absorption band of CDs red-shifts significantly in the range of 200–250 nm (Fig. 2g), providing further evidence of nitration between the CDs and ONOO−. The FL lifetime of CDs remains unchanged before and after the addition of ONOO− (Fig. 2h and Table S3), suggesting that no energy or electron transfer occurs between the CDs and ONOO−, as such processes typically affect FL lifetime [29,30]. Based on the above discussions, the specific response mechanism of ONOO− involves its strong nitration capability to oxidize the amino groups on the surface of the CDs into nitro groups, forming non-fluorescent complexes, resulting the quenching of FL intensity of the CDs. Meanwhile, we have investigated the stability and response characteristics of CDs after 6 months of storage. As shown in Fig. S9, after six months of storage, the CDs maintain their original FL intensity without significant changes, while their response performance toward ONOO− remains stable, demonstrating excellent long-term stability of the CDs.

Construction of electrochemical attosyringe. Conical-shaped nanopipettes are fabricated using a laser pulling method which is described in supporting information, resulting in a tip size of approximately 100 nm (Fig. S10). The stability of the nanopipettes is assessed by measuring electrochemical conductance through I–V curves from-1000 to 1000 mV. As shown in Fig. S11, the I–V curves remain unchanged over 60 min, indicating excellent stability. For single cell imaging using the nanopipette, it is crucial to make sure the viability of living cells after penetration. Propidium iodide (PI) staining analysis revealed no red FL after nanopipette insertion into living cells for 60 min (Fig. S12), indicating that the insertion does not damage the cell membrane or affect cell viability.

Next, single-cell perfusion of CDs is performed using an electrochemical attosyringe [31]. Prior to insertion into HeLa cells, the attosyringe is fixed on a microoperation holder for accurate movement control via an electric driving system (Fig. S13). The culture dish containing HeLa cells is placed on the microscope platform, and the attosyringe tip is carefully guided towards the selected cell. Through fine adjustment, the tip is precisely inserted into the cell membrane (Fig. S14). Before applying voltage to the attosyringe, no FL is detected in the selected cell, confirming that the CDs do not leak from the attosyringe tip. Upon applying varying voltages, different intensities of FL are observed inside the cell (Fig. 3 and S15).

As the applied voltage increased, CDs are effectively injected into the cell, demonstrating a positive correlation between voltage and CDs perfusion. At 500 mV, the cell exhibits significant FL within 5 min. Compared to traditional incubation methods (Fig. S16), the electrochemical attosyringe demonstrate high efficiency and rapid performance. Upon retracting the attosyringe from the cell, the membrane remains intact, and the CDs do not leak out, indicating that the attosyringe do not cause cellular damage, which is beneficial for subsequent in-situ detection.

In-situ detection of ONOO− in a single cell using electrochemical attosyringe. The capability of the electrochemical attosyringe for detecting ONOO− in single living cells is evaluated (Fig. 4 ). In the control group, following a 5-min injection of CDs (Fig. 4, Stage i), a prominent blue FL appears in HeLa cells, indicating successful CDs delivery. The injection amount of CDs for ONOO− detection in a single cell is calculated following our previously reported method [32]. At a voltage of 500 mV, the injection rate is estimated to be 18.3 fL/s (Table S4), implying that the injection volume of CDs utilized in single-cell ONOO− detection is approximately 5.5 pL, corresponding to a 5-min injection. To stimulate ONOO− production, HeLa cells are treated with SIN-1 (1 mM) or a combination of LPS (5 μg/mL) and IFN-γ (1 μg/mL). SIN-1 serves as an ONOO− donor, while LPS and IFN-γ trigger cellular apoptosis and endogenous ONOO− release [33,34]. Upon treatment, the blue FL in the cells is significantly quenched due to the presence of ONOO− (Fig. 4, Stage i). After retracting the nanopipette for 5 min (Fig. 4, Stage ii), the blue FL remain almost unchanged, demonstrating the capability of the electrochemical attosyringe for imaging ONOO− at the single-cell level. In a separate experiment, HeLa cells are treated with LPS/IFN-γ followed by TEMPO (300 μM) showed no FL quenching of CDs, confirming the absence of ONOO−. Similarly, pretreatment with UA followed by SIN-1 incubation show no FL change, indicating the scavenging of ONOO−. These results collectively demonstrate that the electrochemical attosyringe is capable of in-situ detection of ONOO− within a single cell, offering a powerful tool for studying cellular oxidative stress at the single-cell level.

In summary, we have successfully prepared blue-emitting CDs with a specific response to ONOO− and integrated them into a nanopipette to construct an electrochemical attosyringe. The prepared CDs exhibit excellent stability and biocompatibility, enable them suitable for bioimaging and chemical sensing applications. Due to the strong nitration ability of ONOO−, the blue FL of the CDs is efficiently quenched upon interaction with ONOO−. The CDs-based probes demonstrate high sensitivity and selectivity toward ONOO−, with a linear detection range from 0.06 to 1.0 μM. Notably, the developed electrochemical attosyringe enables in-situ visualization of both exogenous and endogenous ONOO− in a single living cell. Compared to traditional incubation methods, the injection method offers significant advantages in terms of efficiency and speed. This study not only achieves in-situ detection of ONOO− at a single-cell level for the first time, but also provides a valuable incubation-free tool for exploring the complex biological function of ONOO− in related pathological processes.

Shuai Chang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ji Fen Shi: Formal analysis. Ya Ting Gao: Investigation. Ming Jie Ye: Investigation. Ruo Can Qian: Project administration. Jian Lv: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Bin Bin Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Da Wei Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22176058), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (24DX1400200, 22ZR1416800, and 23ZR1416100), the Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities (B16017), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (222201717003) and Postdoctoral Foundation Project of Shenzhen Polytechnic University (6024331012K). We thank the Research Center of Analysis and Test of East China University of Science and Technology for help with the characterization.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The superoxide radical switch in the biology of nitric oxide and peroxynitrite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Piacenza, A. Zeida, M. Trujillo, R. Radi, The superoxide radical switch in the biology of nitric oxide and peroxynitrite, Physiol. Rev. 102 (2022) 1881-1906.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive near-infrared imaging of peroxynitrite fluxes in inflammation progress

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, W. Wang, P. Wang, X. Song, Z. Mao, Z. Liu, Highly sensitive near-infrared imaging of peroxynitrite fluxes in inflammation progress, Anal. Chem. 93 (2021) 3035-3041.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tumor-specific peroxynitrite overproduction disrupts metabolic homeostasis for sensitizing melanoma immunotherapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Yang, D. Wang, H. Jia, C. Yang, Y. Zhang, H. Li, J. Liu, J. Liu, Tumor-specific peroxynitrite overproduction disrupts metabolic homeostasis for sensitizing melanoma immunotherapy, Adv. Mater. 35 (2023) 2301455.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Protective role of glutathione against peroxynitrite-mediated DNA damage during acute inflammation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Ahmed, A. Chakrabarty, F.P. Guengerich, G. Chowdhury, Protective role of glutathione against peroxynitrite-mediated DNA damage during acute inflammation, Chem. Res. Toxicol. 33 (2020) 2668-2674.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct assessment of nitrative stress in lipid environments: applications of a designer lipid-based biosensor for peroxynitrite

Izgu, Direct assessment of nitrative stress in lipid environments: Applications of a designer lipid-based biosensor for peroxynitrite, iScience 26 (2023) 108567.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Defense mechanism of phosphorothioated DNA under peroxynitrite-mediated oxidative stress

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Huang, J. Li, T. Shi, J. Liang, Z. Wang, L. Bai, Z. Deng, Y.L. Zhao, Defense mechanism of phosphorothioated DNA under peroxynitrite-mediated oxidative stress, ACS Chem. Biol. 15 (2020) 2558-2567.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activity-based fluorescence probes for pathophysiological peroxynitrite fluxes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On-demand generation of peroxynitrite from an integrated two-dimensional system for enhanced tumor therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Liu, W. Li, H. Chen, J. Zhou, S. Dong, P. Zang, B. Tian, H. Ding, S. Gai, P. Yang, Y. Zhao, On-demand generation of peroxynitrite from an integrated two-dimensional system for enhanced tumor therapy, ACS Nano 16 (2022) 8939-8953.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A chemiluminescent probe for imaging peroxynitrite in inflammatory cells and tissues

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Deng, X. Shi, X. Hu, L. Xu, X. Liu, G. Gao, R. Wang, G. Liang, A chemiluminescent probe for imaging peroxynitrite in inflammatory cells and tissues, Anal. Chem. 95 (2023) 6496-6500.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxygen-embedded pentacene based near-infrared chemiluminescent nanoprobe for highly selective and sensitive visualization of peroxynitrite in vivo

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wang, Y. Wang, Y. Wang, Y. Zhao, C. Yang, Z. Zeng, S. Huan, G. Song, X. Zhang, Oxygen-embedded pentacene based near-infrared chemiluminescent nanoprobe for highly selective and sensitive visualization of peroxynitrite in vivo, Anal. Chem. 92 (2020) 4154-4163.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and applications of fluorescent detectors for peroxynitrite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wang, L. Chen, P. Jangili, A. Sharma, W. Li, J.-T. Hou, C. Qin, J. Yoon, J.S. Kim, Design and applications of fluorescent detectors for peroxynitrite, Coordin. Chem. Rev. 374 (2018) 36-54.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection of peroxynitrite using a biosensor based on a conducting polymer-manganese ion complex

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.C.A. Koh, J.I. Son, E.S. Choe, Y.-B. Shim, Electrochemical detection of peroxynitrite using a biosensor based on a conducting polymer-manganese ion complex, Anal. Chem. 82 (2010) 10075-10082.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel ratiometric electrochemical sensing strategy for monitoring of peroxynitrite anion released from high glucose-induced cells
